titleInfo.title: Zhonghuang Shan kejing yu weishi guxue
中皇山刻經與唯識古學
Stone Carved Sutras of Mount Zhonghuang and the Ancient Consciousness-Only Teaching

mods.abstract: This article first gives a taxonomie of the inscription sites in Shandong, Hebei and Henan of the Northern dynasties and the early Sui period. Next the inscription site of Zhonghuangshan in Shexian is described. The following sutras are engraved: The "Siyi fantian suowen jing 思益凡天所问经" in more than 30.000 charcters; the "Shenmi jietuo jing 深密解脱经" (T. 675.16:668-687, translated in 514) also in more than 30.000 characters; the "Shidi 士地" in more than 60.000 characters; the "Fo yi jiao jing 佛遗教经" and the "Guanyin jing" in 1000 characters each. The second part of the article points out that the selection of texts engarved in Zhonghuangshan is closely connected to "Old Mind Only 古唯识" studies, meaning early Yogacāra Buddhism. The "Shenmi jietuo jing 深密解脱经" that was translated by Bodhiruci is considered such an early Yogacāra work. Conventionally, Xuanzang is held to be the actual founder of the Chinese Weishi school (唯识新学) which is also called Faxiang school. He re-translated the "Shenmi jietuo jing" under the title "Jie shenmi jing 解深密经". Scholars who had already assimilated Indian Yogacāra thought under the Northern and Southern dynasties can be found among the so called "Shelun 摄论师 masters" and the "Dilun masters". The first group refers to scholars of the "She dasheng lun" 摄大乘论Mahāyāna samparigraha-śāstra by Asanga that was translated by Paramārtha. The second group refers to scholars of the "Ten Stages Sūtra" (Daśabhūmikasūtra-śāstra) of which a version is also engraved at Zhonghuangshan. The version of the Ten Stages Sūtra engraved at Zhonghuangshan is not identical with the printed Taishō version (T. 1522.26.123-203) of the Treatise on the Ten Stages Sūtra (Daśabhūmikasūtra-śāstra) by Vasubandhu that was translated into Chinese by Bodhiruci and Ratnamati in the year 512. The Zhonghuangshan Ten Stages Sūtra relies on the sūtra part of the Treatise on the Ten Stages Sūtra, but also adds passages from the The Sūtra on the Ten Stages (十住經), an earlier translation by Kumārajīva (T. 286.10.497-535), and verses taken from the Ten Stages Chapter 十地品(fascicle 23 to 27) of the first Chinese translation of the Āvatamsaka-sūtra (T. 278.09.542-578) in 60 fascicles by Buddhabhadra of the Eastern Jin dynasty from 418-420. The third part of the article deals with the southern and northern branch of the Dilun school. It discusses various theories about the meaning of the denominations of "northern" and "southern" and the location of "Xiangzhou" (the capitel Ye or Anyang?) and introduces the scholar monks associated with both traditions.

name.namePart: 山東省石刻藝術博物館
